claims.claim: Enterprise AI has surged from $1.7B to $37B since 2023, now capturing 6% of the global SaaS market.
claims.speaker: Menlo Ventures
claims.comparison_note: Menlo's $37B Enterprise GenAI spend covers infra + apps + foundation models; maps roughly to Enterprise + SME buyer segments plus part of capex.

triangulation.derivation: Menlo says Enterprise GenAI = $37B (apps $19B + infra $18B). Our model: Enterprise buyer ($10.1B) + SME buyer ($3.8B) = $13.9B in customer revenue. Adding enterprise direct capex ($15B) gets $28.9B. Gap to $37B (~$8B) likely reflects Menlo's inclusion of foundation model API spend routed through enterprises that we attribute to providers via Hyperscaler/API channels. Implied: enterprise+SME segment may be undercounted by ~$5-8B, OR Menlo includes scope our ledger excludes (training infra, orchestration tools).

claims.source_excerpt:
nd side tells a different story: Our latest market data shows broad adoption, real revenue, and productivity gains at scale, signaling a boom versus a bubble. 
Enterprise AI has surged from $1.7B to $**37**B since 2023, now capturing 6% of the global SaaS market and growing faster than any software category in history.
For Menlo’s third annual State of Generative AI in the Enterprise report, we surveyed

claims.claim: AI Applications captured $19 billion in 2025, more than half of all generative AI spending.
claims.speaker: Menlo Ventures
claims.comparison_note: Maps to AI Native Apps + Trad. SaaS channels in our model.

triangulation.derivation: Menlo: AI applications = $19B in 2025. Our model: AI Native Apps ($0.7B) + Trad. SaaS ($2.6B) = $3.3B. Even adding Model Subs ($7.1B, since copilots like ChatGPT Enterprise are subs), gets $10.4B. Gap to $19B is ~$8.6B — suggests our AI Native Apps + Trad. SaaS channels significantly undercount the application layer, OR Menlo's $19B includes spend on copilots/subs we route through Model Subs channel.

claims.claim: Foundation model APIs captured $12.5 billion in 2025.
claims.speaker: Menlo Ventures
claims.comparison_note: Menlo's foundation model API spend more than 2x our Model API channel total.

triangulation.derivation: Menlo: foundation model APIs = $12.5B. Our Model API channel = $5.7B. Delta: +$6.8B / +119%. Likely Menlo includes API spend routed via hyperscaler channels (e.g., Anthropic via Bedrock, OpenAI via Azure). Adding Hyperscalers ($3.6B) gets $9.3B, still short. Suggests our Model API + Hyperscalers channels collectively undercount enterprise foundation model spend by ~$3B.

Model training infrastructure ($4.0 billion) enables frontier labs and enterprises to train and adapt models.

claims.dedup_note: Menlo's foundation model API spend more than 2x our Model API channel total.

claims.claim: Anthropic now earns 40% of enterprise LLM spend, up from 24% last year and 12% in 2023.
claims.speaker: Menlo Ventures
claims.comparison_note: Menlo enterprise LLM share for Anthropic at 40% triangulates our provider revenue split.

triangulation.derivation: Menlo: Anthropic 40%, OpenAI 27%, Google 21% of enterprise LLM spend. In our ledger across 2025 provider revenue, Anthropic $7.3B / OpenAI $13.7B / Google $2.9B = total $23.9B. Anthropic share = 7.3/23.9 = 31%; OpenAI = 57%; Google = 12%. Menlo's enterprise-only view shows Anthropic ahead, ours (all-segment) shows OpenAI dominant. Reconcilable: OpenAI's $13.7B is heavily Consumer/SME via subs; in pure enterprise API, Anthropic likely leads. Suggests our Anthropic enterprise allocation is consistent but our channel routing should weight Anthropic Model API higher.

vative. When we account for “shadow AI adoption”—employees using personal credit cards for tools like ChatGPT Plus, where roughly 27% of usage is work-related6—PLG-driven tools may represent close to **40**% of application AI spend.

claims.claim: Coding is the largest departmental AI category at $4.0 billion (55% of departmental AI spend), up from $550M in 2024.
claims.speaker: Menlo Ventures
claims.comparison_note: Coding tools market triangulates against our coding-focused app entities.

triangulation.derivation: Menlo: AI coding tools = $4.0B in 2025. In our ledger summing 2025 ARR for coding apps: Cursor $1.0B + GitHub Copilot $1.65B + Cognition $0.155B + Replit $0.252B + Lovable $0.4B = $3.46B. Plus Anthropic Claude Code direct (not separately tracked) likely $0.3-0.5B. Total ~$3.8-4.0B aligns with Menlo's $4.0B. Strengthens our coding app ARRs.

across all functions.

Departmental AI spending hit $7.3 billion in 2025, up 4.1x year over year. Coding is the clear standout at $**4.0** billion (55% of departmental AI spend), making it the largest category across the entire application layer; the rest spans IT (10%), marketing (9%), customer success (9%), design (7%), and HR (5%).

claims.claim: The medical scribe market reached $600 million in 2025, +2.4x YoY.
claims.speaker: Menlo Ventures
claims.comparison_note: Scribe market triangulates against Abridge ARR which is currently low-confidence.

triangulation.derivation: Menlo: scribe market = $600M in 2025, with Abridge + Ambience as new unicorns alongside leader Nuance DAX. If Nuance DAX has ~50% share = $300M, and Abridge + Ambience split ~$200M (= $100M each), plus tail $100M. This implies Abridge ARR ~$100M in 2025, vs our ledger's $50M. Delta: +$50M / +100%. Suggests our abridge.2025.arr=0.05 [low] is undercounted by roughly 2x.

claims.dedup_note: Scribe market triangulates against Abridge ARR which is currently low-confidence.

triangulation.derivation: Menlo: copilot spend = $7.2B in 2025 (ChatGPT Enterprise, Claude for Work, MS Copilot). In our ledger Model Subs channel = $7.1B. These are essentially the same construct. Match within 2%. Strongly corroborates our Model Subs sizing.

claims.claim: Startups captured 63% of AI app market share, up from 36% last year.
claims.speaker: Menlo Ventures
claims.comparison_note: Startup vs incumbent split; informational.
claims.source_excerpt:
ants Gain Ground in AI Apps

At the AI application layer, startups have pulled decisively ahead. This year, according to our data, they captured nearly $2 in revenue for every $1 earned by incumbents—**63**% of the market, up from 36% last year7 when enterprises still held the lead.

On paper, this shouldn’t be happening. Incumbents have entrenched distribution, data moats, deep enterprise relationships
claims.dedup_note: Startup vs incumbent split; informational.

claims.speaker: Menlo Ventures
claims.comparison_note: Market structure context.
claims.source_excerpt:
ce, where reliability and deep integrations outweigh speed.
The story changes as we move down the stack. At the infrastructure layer, the picture is more mixed. According to our data, incumbents hold **56**% of the market as many AI app builders continue building on the data platforms they’ve trusted for years. Although new AI-native infrastructure companies like Temporal, Supabase, Neon*, and Pinecone*
claims.dedup_note: Market structure context.
